testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature of the testamentary act, the nature and extent of their property, and the persons who are the natural objects of their bounty
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (Ala. Code § 43-8-131)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (Ala. Code § 43-8-134)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Ala. Code § 43-8-132)

electronic_wills.notes: Alabama does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Alabama

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Alabama. Alabama repealed its nuncupative will statute

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in their presence

special_provisions: Alabama is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. Alabama adopted provisions based on the Uniform Probate Code
statute_citations: Ala. Code § 43-8-130 (Who may make a will)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound and disposing mind and memory
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of both witnesses, and both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (Ark. Code § 28-25-103)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will but may create a presumption of undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Ark. Code § 28-25-106)

electronic_wills.notes: Arkansas does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if the entire will is in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required (Ark. Code § 28-25-104)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Arkansas

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Arkansas is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has dower and curtesy rights: life estate in one-third of real property. Arkansas also provides a statutory allowance and homestead rights to the surviving spouse
statute_citations: Ark. Code § 28-25-101 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (Fla. Stat. § 732.501)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind, understanding the nature and extent of the property to be disposed of, the relationship to those who would naturally claim a benefit, and the practical effect of the will
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed or printed. Florida is strict about formalities

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction. The signature must be at the end of the will (Fla. Stat. § 732.502)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of both witnesses. Both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator and in the presence of each other (Fla. Stat. § 732.502)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will. Florida repealed the interested witness penalty (Fla. Stat. § 732.504)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public or other authorized officer (Fla. Stat. § 732.503)

electronic_wills.notes: Florida adopted the Electronic Wills Act effective January 1, 2020 (Fla. Stat. § 732.521-732.528). Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, electronically signed by the testator, and witnessed by two persons who may be present by audio-video communication if a supervised by a notary

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Florida, even if valid where executed

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Florida

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical act of burning, tearing, canceling, defacing, obliterating, or destroying the will by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence
A writing executed with will formalities

special_provisions: Florida is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share of 30% of the elective estate (Fla. Stat. § 732.2065). Florida has strong homestead protections: the homestead cannot be devised away from a surviving spouse or minor children. Florida has no state income tax or estate tax
statute_citations: Fla. Stat. § 732.501 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Georgia has one of the lowest minimum ages to make a will in the nation (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-10)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must have sufficient intellect to enable them to have a decided and rational desire as to the disposition of property and to understand the nature of the testamentary act
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-20)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of the witnesses, and the witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-20)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-23)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-24)

electronic_wills.notes: Georgia enacted electronic will provisions effective July 1, 2022. Electronic wills must be created, signed, and witnessed electronically and stored by a qualified custodian

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in Georgia

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last illness, in the testator's dwelling or where they resided for 10+ days (except sudden illness), before two competent witnesses, and limited to $100 unless reduced to writing within 30 days (O.C.G.A. § 53-4-27)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction by the testator or at the testator's direction
Any writing executed with the same formalities as a will

special_provisions: Georgia is a separate property/common law state. Georgia has NO elective share statute — a surviving spouse can be disinherited. However, a surviving spouse can petition for a year's support from the estate (O.C.G.A. § 53-3-1). Georgia's minimum age of 14 is the lowest in the nation. Georgia is one of the few states without elective share protections

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and over 18 years old (KRS 394.020)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (KRS 394.040)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two credible witnesses who must subscribe in the testator's presence (KRS 394.040)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise or bequest to a subscribing witness is void unless there are two other disinterested subscribing witnesses (KRS 394.210)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (KRS 394.225)

electronic_wills.notes: Kentucky does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. Must be proven by two witnesses to the testator's handwriting (KRS 394.040)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Kentucky

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction

special_provisions: Kentucky is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has a dower/curtesy interest and may renounce the will and take a statutory share (KRS 392.020). Kentucky also has a state inheritance tax (rates vary by class of beneficiary). Kentucky is one of only a few states with both an inheritance tax and dower rights

minimum_age.exceptions: Persons 16 or older may make a will (La. C.C. art. 1476)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must have the mental capacity to understand the nature of the testamentary act, the nature and extent of their property, and the relationship to the persons who are the natural objects of their bounty. Must not be interdicted (La. C.C. art. 1477)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Louisiana recognizes two forms: notarial testament and olographic (holographic) testament. Mystic testaments were abolished in 2025

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: For notarial testament: testator must sign at the end and on each separate page. If unable to sign, testator must make a mark and declare inability to sign. For olographic testament: testator must sign at the end (La. C.C. art. 1577, 1579)

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be competent and at least 16 years old. For notarial testament, a notary is also required in addition to witnesses
witness_requirements.presence_rules: For notarial testament: testator must sign in the presence of a notary and two witnesses. The testator must declare or signify that the instrument is their will. Witnesses and notary must sign at the end (La. C.C. art. 1577). For olographic testament: no witnesses required
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: Interested witnesses do not invalidate the will but may be examined more closely

notarization.notes: Required for notarial testament — must be executed before a notary and two witnesses. Not required for olographic testament (La. C.C. art. 1577)

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: A notarial testament is self-proving by its nature — the notary's attestation serves this function. No separate affidavit needed (La. C.C. art. 1577)

electronic_wills.notes: Louisiana does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Called 'olographic testament' in Louisiana. Must be entirely written, dated, and signed by the testator's own hand. No witnesses or notary required (La. C.C. art. 1575)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in Louisiana. Nuncupative testaments were abolished

methods: Executing a subsequent testament that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Physical destruction by the testator with the intention of revoking
revocation.notes: La. C.C. art. 1607. Louisiana also allows revocation by a signed declaration before a notary and two witnesses

special_provisions: Louisiana is a community property state and uses civil law (not common law) for succession. Louisiana has FORCED HEIRSHIP: children 23 or younger, or children of any age who are permanently incapable of managing their affairs, are forced heirs entitled to a portion of the estate (La. C.C. art. 1493). The forced portion is one-quarter of the estate if one forced heir, one-half if two or more. Louisiana's succession law is rooted in French/Spanish civil law traditions and is unique among US states. Act No. 30 of 2025 relaxed some formal requirements for notarial testaments
statute_citations: La. C.C. art. 1476 (Capacity to make a will)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound and disposing mind and memory
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.qualifications: Must be credible witnesses at least 18 years old
witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two or more credible witnesses who shall subscribe their names in the testator's presence (Miss. Code § 91-5-1)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise to an attesting witness is void unless the will is otherwise properly witnessed by two disinterested witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary or other authorized officer (Miss. Code § 91-5-1)

electronic_wills.notes: Mississippi does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly written and signed by the testator in the testator's own handwriting. No witnesses required. Must be proven by at least two credible witnesses to the handwriting (Miss. Code § 91-5-1)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last sickness, at the testator's habitual residence or where they fell sick, before two competent witnesses. Value limited to $100 for personal property unless proved by two witnesses that the testator called on someone to take notice (Miss. Code § 91-5-15)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in their presence

special_provisions: Mississippi is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. Mississippi's nuncupative will limit of $100 for personal property is extremely restrictive. Mississippi also has a homestead exemption for the surviving spouse
statute_citations: Miss. Code § 91-5-1 (Execution of wills; holographic wills; self-proving)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (N.C.G.S. § 31-1)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may subscribe the testator's name in the testator's presence and at the testator's direction (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.3)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two witnesses, and both witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.3)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (N.C.G.S. § 31-10)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (N.C.G.S. § 31-11.6)

electronic_wills.notes: North Carolina does not currently recognize electronic wills, though NC enacted provisions for electronic storage of paper wills effective January 2026

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if written entirely in the testator's handwriting, signed by the testator, and found after death among the testator's valuable papers or effects, or in a safe-deposit box, or in the custody of some person with whom it was deposited for safekeeping (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.4)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made during last sickness or in imminent peril of death, before two competent witnesses. Only valid for personal property up to a limited value. Must be proved within 6 months after the speaking of the alleged testamentary words (N.C.G.S. § 31-3.5)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: North Carolina is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share ranging from a life estate in one-third to an outright share depending on the number of surviving descendants (N.C.G.S. § 30-3.1). North Carolina's holographic will must be found among valuable papers — unique requirement. North Carolina has no state estate tax

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (S.C. Code § 62-2-501)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's conscious presence and by the testator's direction (S.C. Code § 62-2-502)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: Each witness must sign within a reasonable time after witnessing the signing or the testator's acknowledgment of the signature or the will
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (S.C. Code § 62-2-505)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (S.C. Code § 62-2-504)

electronic_wills.notes: South Carolina does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Holographic wills are not recognized in South Carolina

nuncupative_wills.notes: Oral wills are not recognized in South Carolina

methods: Executing a subsequent will that revokes the prior will expressly or by inconsistency
Performing a revocatory act (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying) by the testator or by another in the testator's conscious presence and at their direction

special_provisions: South Carolina is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share of one-third of the probate estate (S.C. Code § 62-2-201). South Carolina adopted the Uniform Probate Code
statute_citations: S.C. Code § 62-2-501 (Who may make a will)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (Tenn. Code § 32-1-102)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction (Tenn. Code § 32-1-104)

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two witnesses, and the witnesses must sign in the presence of the testator (Tenn. Code § 32-1-104)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will but may create an inference of undue influence

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Tenn. Code § 32-2-110)

electronic_wills.notes: Tennessee does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution but must be proven by two witnesses to the handwriting (Tenn. Code § 32-1-105)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: must be made in imminent peril of death, before two disinterested witnesses, and only for personal property not exceeding $1,000 in value. Must be reduced to writing within 20 days and submitted for probate within 6 months (Tenn. Code § 32-1-106)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Tennessee is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share (Tenn. Code § 31-4-101). Tennessee has no state income tax (Hall Tax repealed 2021) and no state estate tax. Tennessee allows a real property disposition separate from the will via a recorded deed
statute_citations: Tenn. Code § 32-1-102 (Who may make a will)

minimum_age.exceptions: Emancipated minors may make a will (Va. Code § 64.2-401)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind (Va. Code § 64.2-401)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign the testator's name in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two competent witnesses who are present at the same time. Witnesses must sign in the testator's presence (Va. Code § 64.2-403)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: An interested witness does not invalidate the will (Va. Code § 64.2-404)

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (Va. Code § 64.2-405)

electronic_wills.notes: Virginia does not currently have an electronic wills statute

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution. Must be proved by two disinterested witnesses to the handwriting (Va. Code § 64.2-403)

nuncupative_wills.notes: Virginia abolished nuncupative wills effective July 1, 2007

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical act (cutting, tearing, burning, obliterating, canceling, or destroying) by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: Virginia is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share: if no surviving descendants, one-half of the augmented estate; if surviving descendants, one-third of the augmented estate (Va. Code § 64.2-302). Virginia requires witnesses to be present at the same time, which is stricter than some states
statute_citations: Va. Code § 64.2-401 (Who may make a will)

testamentary_capacity: The testator must be of sound mind and not under undue influence (W. Va. Code § 41-1-1)
writing_requirement: Must be in writing. Can be typed, printed, or handwritten

signature_requirement.proxy_signing_rules: Another person may sign in the testator's presence and by the testator's express direction

witness_requirements.presence_rules: The testator must sign or acknowledge the will in the presence of two competent witnesses who are present at the same time. Witnesses must sign in the testator's presence (W. Va. Code § 41-1-3)
witness_requirements.interested_witness_rules: A devise to an attesting witness is void unless there are two other disinterested witnesses

notarization.notes: Not required for validity; used for self-proving affidavit

self_proving_affidavit.requirements: Testator and witnesses sign a sworn affidavit before a notary public (W. Va. Code § 41-5-15)

electronic_wills.notes: West Virginia enacted electronic will provisions (W. Va. Code § 41-1-4a). Electronic wills must be in an electronic record, signed electronically by the testator, and witnessed

holographic_wills.notes: Valid if wholly in the testator's handwriting and signed by the testator. No witnesses required at execution. Must be proved by two witnesses to the handwriting

nuncupative_wills.notes: Recognized in limited circumstances: only for military personnel in actual military service and mariners at sea (W. Va. Code § 41-1-5)

methods: Executing a subsequent will or codicil
Physical destruction (burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating) by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence

special_provisions: West Virginia is a separate property/common law state. Surviving spouse has an elective share. West Virginia requires witnesses to be present at the same time
statute_citations: W. Va. Code § 41-1-1 (Who may make a will)
